comments.title: Voluspa Performance and Request for Well Wishes
comments.content:
Good Evening!

I hope others had a similar combination of pleasant weather, striking sunset, and a prominent Full Moon rising that we enjoyed here.

To Share: I ran across Kersti Ståbi through Rune Rasmussen's "Nordic Animist Calendar". She did her PhD thesis on what the oral performances of Eddic poems like the Voluspa might have sounded like. To arrive at this, she studied what active oral epic/poetic traditions she could along with the metre of Eddic poems, and then experimented through performances. She collects some links to recordings of her performances here: https://kerstistabi.com/eddavoluspa

To Ask: For JMG and the commentariat: tomorrow (or today, depending on when you read this), on Monday, my wife is scheduled to bring our second daughter into the world via C-Section. For anyone willing, any and all prayers, well wishes, and good energy for the safety and comfort of mother and baby would be most welcome. Obviously, I may be a bit behind on posting thank you's, but I'll be sure to share an update soon!

As always, thanks to JMG for maintaining this space and to the commentariat for providing such stimulating conversation.

Cheers,
Jeff

comments.content:
Thanks very much for hosting these as always, JMG!

For a little over a year now, I’ve been DMing a Dungeons & Dragons campaign for my middle school aged son and his friends. I agreed to do it because, hey, I enjoy being the fun dad as much as the next guy… but what I’ve witnessed take place over the last 14 months or so has been nothing short of remarkable. I think I’m watching four friends guide each other through a psychological and spiritual initiation process that on many occasions has made me imagine C.G. Jung or Joseph Campbell giving me a knowing smile and a wink at my kitchen table. All I’ve had to do is provide them with the symbolic language of story as backdrop…

All this has led me to wonder: has anyone done any writing on the occult or spiritual aspects of TTRPGs? I remember from a while back that you published a TTRPG connected with your Weird of Hali novels, but I can’t recall if you’ve written on this subject.

Best,
Ryan M.

And yes, the Weird of Hali roleplaying game is a thing -- a rugose, squamous thing, of course. If anyone's interested, details are here.

I appreciate the suggestion to write up an account of my experiences. I’d be all ears if you or one of your readers know of a publication that would consider such a piece—I’m not sure where I’d find a home for something along the lines of “we live in a culture that’s increasingly bereft of rites of passage or serious religious initiations, but never fear: in my experience as a parent, given a fantasy universe rich in mythopoetic possibility and enough agency to chart their own course in said universe, a group of precocious preteens is more than capable of tapping into the Hero’s Journey and all its various archetypes and will give themselves what modern western society has deprived them of…” 😁

In the meantime, I’ve focused on extolling the educational benefits of these games. I had the mom of one of the other boys tell me that his teacher can’t get him to write a full paragraph to save her own life, but after one session of D&D he took it upon himself to write three pages of backstory for his character. There are obvious spiritual implications there too—I believe the lad has been able to tap into some flow of Awen through the game and has ignited a bit of his own inner Bard—but, it’s easier to get parents to listen when you bring up academic benefits.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Not exactly esoteric in nature, but if you're looking for some deeper looks into RPGs and what they're up to, a few things that might be helpful:

- Playing At the World: A book by Jon Peterson, this explores the pre- and early history of D&D as a game and a hobby. Extremely well-researched, to the point of academic dry-ness at times, this book is fantastic for highlighting some of the deeper implications of the games, literature, and other influences that shaped early D&D, and how some of that might have lingered in the DNA of the game.
- Vincent Baker: My personal favorite of the "post-Forge Diaspora" game designers, best known for "Apocalypse World", which has spawned a whole "Powered by the Apocalypse" niche of a niche of game design. "The Forge" (http://www.indie-rpgs.com/forge/index.php) was an online community for the discussion of RPG design that is now closed, but which still hosts the archives of the community's discussions. These are folks who picked up games like "Vampire: the Masquerade" that promised to deliver compelling "stories" and were disappointed when it just felt like whatever D&D was doing, but with different spells/powers/weapons. I've since grown less interested in this particular sub-genre (see below for where my current interests lie), but if you are struck by the emergence of archetypes and meaningful storylines, these guys are laser-focused on how to make games more reliably produce recognizable traditional story narratives. If that's what you're looking for out of your RPGs, they do a pretty good job, and Baker does an even better job than most of his peers and imitators.
- The Old School Renaissance: My own "home scene" in terms of what blogs I read, what folks I talk to online, and so forth, when it comes to D&D, and for the last 10 years or so. This style of play and design started when folks looked back at the D&D of the late 70's and 80's and said "hey, they had something right there!" and have explored what that "something right" might have been - whether in rules, setting, playstyle, or otherwise. Perhaps most relevant to the comment here, a big idea in the OSR is "emergent narrative" - the idea that if you put forward challenging situation and throw in random characters who may or may not be heroic, over time, you'll get interesting, meaningful storylines that grow out of the creative problem-solving of the players and the random outcomes of the dice. Such un-planned narratives likely can't help but be archetypal in nature, since they're being shaped by human beings and their interaction with a mix of random and predictable challenges.

Anyhow, this is already long, so I'll stop here, but if any of this has intrigued you or you'd like to learn more, let me know.

I hope some of these prove fruitful for you, but in the end, running an ongoing game that you as the DM and your players keep finding worth showing up for is the most important thing. Best wishes for your campaign!

Cheers,
Jeff

comments.title: Letters
comments.content:
The more I learn about letter mysticism and various forms of divination the more they seem deeply interconnected. The approach you have suggested in the Levi posts for each tarot card is very similar to the approach I see advocated for runes and connects with the ideas in Kolb’s Buchstabenbuch, where Kolb emphasises the idea of the letter as being more like a Platonic form and should not be confused with the written form. Indeed he describes nature as being constructed from these 'letters' (ie. the word of God). So it increasingly seems to me that the Tarot, Ogham, Runes, letters, numbers, etc. are to some extent (i.e. adjusting for the different numbers) external representations of the same underlying forms and that connecting with them is a fairly common approach towards developing the self in the Western esoteric tradition. Does this make sense?

- Long aside with no question:
Actually I am also wondering whether anyone has directly connected parts of these traditions with the concept in literary theory of intertextuality (Julia Kristeva is the most prominent theorist in this area, developing the ideas of Bakhtin). I suspect not, though there may be an indirect link through Jung to Kristeva. Per Kristeva: "any text is constructed as a mosaic of quotations and is the absorption and transformation of another text". This is generally interpreted as words and phrases drawing meaning from previous uses in other texts. A word can denote something, but it also brings connotations (which may vary from reader to reader). To my mind modern literary theory has also taken a mystical turn disguised in a way not dissimilar to that of old alchemical texts, and I expect many works of modern literary theory may end up being looked on in a similar way (if they're not already).

replies.title: Re: Letters
replies.content:
1) Yes, it makes perfect sense. The Idea (in Plato's sense) of an alphabetic mysticism setting out basic elements of existence takes form in various instantiations in practice, just as the Idea of a table takes form in various instantiations, which can be made of different materials, have different numbers of legs, and so on. Have you by any chance read Paolo Rossi's Logic and the Art of Memory? He discusses the Renaissance version of this, which attempted to create an alphabet of fundamental concepts from which all human thought could be constructed.

2) I've never seen anyone make that connection, but then I don't follow current literary theory at all and a dozen writers could have been all over it for all I'd know.

comments.content:
Two weeks ago I posted a speculation I've had, that there may be some evidence to suggest the laws of the material plane shift when new planets come into focus, and that this may mean that a lot of modern technology may stop working in the near future as Pluto fades out. I've realized that there is in fact a good way to test this hypothesis: if this happened, then there should be evidence of this happening in the Romantic Era. What this means then is that there may be Cererian technologies which only worked during the Romantic Era, while Ceres was a planet. Given Ceres is a lot less focused on technology than Pluto is, they may be subtle, and quiet, but I have begun the process of looking for evidence of this.

One other possible avenue for investigation has occurred to me, however, and this is that if the laws of physics are reverting to normal, the rate at which Plutonian technologies fail will likely begin to increase, and there is some evidence of this happening, in a variety of forms: the failures of modern medicine; the odd issues with LEDs reported in some cities; the anecdotal reports I've heard from people of various synthetic drugs (ex: LSD) not causing the same effects they used to; or the odd way that so many aircraft have malfunctioned or vanished over the past few years, which is odd given they are, in theory, a mature technology.

Teasing out which of these are signs of the underlying laws of physics changing, and which are merely because of a lack of maintenance and the obsession with the new over old and proven tech is rather difficult, but then again a fog of deception, fraud, and confounding factors hiding important truths is a common Plutonian pattern...

replies.content:
One thing in computer technology that comes to mind is the Year 2038 problem. This is when the 32-bit signed time counter variable rolls over from its maximum positive (2^31-1 = 2147483647, a little more than 2 billion) to its maximum negative (-2^31) value. As this is the number of seconds since midnight 1970-01-01 (the «Unix Epoch») clocks will roll from 2038-01-19 03:14:07 to 1901-01-13 20:45:52.

And 1901 to 2038 are close to the ends of the Plutonian era, though this symmetry isn't surprising seeing that that the Unix Epoch of 1970 is near the middle point of it. Still, the combination of this and the 32-bit second counter makes this look like an eerie coincidence, if not more than that. Who would have known that the duration of the Plutonian era was going to be close to 2^32 seconds (approximately 4.3 billion seconds).

replies.title: Re: Killing other creatures
replies.content:
First of all, the way of nature includes killing. Death is an inescapable part of life, and every living thing lives on the remains of other living things -- even plants send their roots through the soil looking for nutrients that were once the bodies of plants and animals. You're not doing anything with the ants that an aardvark doesn't do to them when it tears an ant nest open with its strong claws and feeds itself and its cubs on the tasty contents.

Second, you can certainly explain matters to the ants. Sometime when you see a bunch of them, sit down and talk to them. Explain to them why what they're doing isn't something you can allow, explain to them that you're going to have pest control in to kill them, tell them that any of them that want to survive should take some queen and drone eggs and go someplace far away, and ask them to take that message back to their queen and the other ants. Some of them may take your advice. Such things happen surprisingly often!

comments.title: Today's Druidry and Theosophy
comments.content:
Good Morning, JMG. How much of today's druidry as practiced by your branch and by other branches includes as part of its (re)construction concepts that found their way into druidry's body of knowledge and practice via Theosophy as purveyed by Madame Blavatsky and her associates? I recall you writing that so much of original druidry was lost through successive waves of Roman and Christian persecution back in the bad old days that when the time came to rebuild, the rebuilders had to take from from a lot of sources. I've tried some independent research online regarding this and haven't been very successful. But I imagine concepts like karma may have become part of druidry as practiced nowadays via HP Blavatsky's writings.

Any light you can shed on this will be much appreciated by me,
Casey. I thank you.

replies.title: Re: Today's Druidry and Theosophy
replies.content:
Theosophy is a latecomer compared to the Druid Revival. The first documented Druid organizations in modern times were going by the mid-1700s -- tradition has it that the oldest of them was founded in 1717 -- and there was a Druid presence in Britain, Ireland, and the newly founded United States before 1800. Iolo Morganwg, whose abundant writings include the entire Druid teaching on reincarnation, died in 1826; Blavatsky was born in 1831. I use terms like "karma" because most people nowadays understand them, and because the equivalent concept in older Druid writings doesn't have a convenient one-word label.

If you want to know where the Druid Revival got many of its ideas, I recommend a good hard look at Greek Neoplatonism, Pythagoreanism, and Hermeticism. Those were all in circulation well before the Revival and many significant figures in the Revival's early days were well aware of them.

replies.title: Re: Today's Druidry and Theosophy
replies.content:

replies.title: Re: On Intention
replies.content: It depends on how specific your intention is! I'm sorry that I can't be more precise than that. Try "a love partner with whom I will establish a happy and enduring relationship" or something like that, and leave the details up to the cosmos. It's fascinating to me how many people think they know what they want in a lover or a spouse, and are completely wrong!

replies.title: Re: Queen Elizabeth II and the Occult
replies.content:
I've seen no evidence whatsoever that the late Queen was a mage. She did, however, fill a specific magical role in society -- that's what monarchy is, when it's disencumbered from issues of political power. (Monarchy, leadership by one person, is distinct from monocracy, the concentration of power in the hands of one person.) A monarch's job is to serve as the magical representation of humanity within a given nation, and the late Queen did a fine job of that.

As for the quote, er, you do know that most religions also teach that, don't you?

replies.title: Re: Sylphs
replies.content:
1) That's like asking what humans are in terms of personalities. Every sylph I've ever encountered had a different personality.

2) Any information or assistance relating to the element of air is within their power. What they get in return is the chance to interact with a being of more than one element, and this is crucial to their evolution: as they interact with us, they pick up the capacity to interact with the other three elements, and evolve a soul.

3) Systematic magical work with all four elements is involved here -- it's not wise to unbalance yourself by working with just one. You need to learn to scry the realms of the elements and interact with the beings in them.

comments.content:
I have been reading Hamlet's Mill, and find myself wondering why it opens with the original revenge story of Amleth the Dane, and its counterpart in other cultures. It seems to me to have nothing to do with astronomy, and everything to do with the politics of royal houses in certain sorts of societies. Now, the great mill that grinds out abundance, salt, or whatever, yes, that's clearly the turning of the heavens on their axis. But a younger royal brother killing the incumbent, usurping the throne, courting or marrying the widow, and the dispossessed prince pretending to be a fool as a cover for his plans and in order to be sage from his uncle - not in a society with cops and law and order, except as a look back at a more barbarian past (i.e. Egypt's tale of the gods acting out this plot) - but most decidedly in early period Germanic society. And as we see, Polynesian, and many, many others with a similar structure and history of private vengeance.

However, trying to tease out the threads of "this refers to what's going on in the heavens" is a marvelous workout for the mind.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The story of Hamlet is one version of the myth that tells of the end of one age of the world and the beginning of another. Of course it's fitted out with political and psychological details, because there's something that de Santillana and von Dechend never really grasped, which is that mythology is not just about one thing. Myth is an information storage technology, and a very rich and subtle one; any given myth is meant to be unpacked in multiple ways, and to yield many different sets of parallel information content.

Sri Arya once quipped on a youtube vid the Shakti gets stuck because the prana is - no joke, his words - "literally in a traffic jam."

Sri Arya also says a special meaning of the card: Recovery from long illness. Unexpected promotions for military people.

Jun: The Star - Hope. Suit tattva: space (akasha) card tattva: all. Karma type: Purushartha (willpower born karma). Karma quality: Adridha (fluid/easily side-stepped)

A time for hope and peace. Healing and reorientation of life directions. Healing is indicated and necessary with this card. It is superlative with spiritual energy. In fact, the spiritual quality of the card is so high that the material, worldly rewards are moderate at best. Search for new jobs is indicated by this card. New levels of trust and comfort in a relationship. Ideal love of a sisterly quality, not romantic one. Feeling in touch with something larger than oneself. Don't go looking for loads of money. Genuine new hope. This card is an 'inner quality' card. A heartening and joyful card. Success is certain but not just yet.

I read this as the U.S. citizenry themselves will step up to the plate and begin taking responsibility to set things aright. Most likely because it will start to dawn on enough people that despite all those government bureaucracies that's not going to be enough to save them. It may not even be the most intelligent way to respond to the unfolding emergencies. They're going to have to save themselves.


This card says many will rise to the occasion and begin to do exactly that - they'll help themselves and their fellow Americans. Here is one more pole-axe (from a surprising angle) taken to the post WW2 belief that only certified centralized technocratic-insight has enough intelligence to set things right. Having said that the kind of help offered will be more powerful in the spiritual and ideological sense than the material one. Material help, though offered, will be moderate at best.

Jul: 4 of Cups - Suit Tattva: water Card Tattva: air of water

Karma Type: Purushartha (actions born of will) Karma Quality: dridha (fixed/hard to side-step)

The ramifications of what happened in early 2023 keep unfolding. Times get even harder. This is the card of conservatism, self-survival take center stage.

Sri Arya says this card also indicates "phenomenal creativity can be unleashed - but only if the person gets his head screwed on right - a really ironical situation. Balancing thinking with acting takes center stage. Tapping into inner wisdom. Recognising external disruptive forces and overcoming/avoiding them. At a very high level of self-awareness, this is a highly spiritual card as all options except spiritual growth get discarded. It's also an early warning of immanent, complete breakdown.[my quick note: which my spread indicates happens in Sep 2023 - 10 of Swords - the rock bottom card, now back to Sri Arya...] Opportunities and possibilities galore but normally blind to them."

Sri Arya then writes at the bottom of the page -

Special insight of the card: What is the higher purpose or spiritual lesson in this stage or episode? Do not let depression overwhelm you.

Since the karma quality is Dridha I think some very hard karma is going to burn hot this month. If my hunch is right I think it's going to be apparent to everyone but GovCorp elites the U.S. will be in the grip of a full-on stagflationary depression. Actually I think even the GovCorp elites will see it but can't publicly admit it. To do so would be instant political suicide (possibly even RL-suicidal).

And here is where I started posting the remaining months for 2023 over on the other blog which can be read here >

https://www.ecosophia.net/futures-that-work/#comments


****Decided to re-post the final months here anyway from the other blog*****

Aug: The Wild Card - Sri Arya's version of the Fool
new potential turning up even in the subtle planes for anyone and any actions taken. Karma type: All/None, Karma Quality: All/None

Sep: 10 of Swords

Sri Arya writes:

This is the rock bottom card, so there is no way but up. The real enemy is within – self-doubt, belittling oneself, sabotaging oneself, pitying oneself. People change when they have to, not when they want to. Now you have to. The closure of things that have outlived their karma – relationships, work, places of residence. Hopefully the power to fight and overthrow evil forces but only if backed by other cards. Spiritually the death of the Old Man, so the new being is freed. New levels of awareness and wakefulness. Gathering up one’s reserves and resources. Recommitment to the struggle. Warning that you missed your life’s purpose, your entelechy. Irrevocable endings. Illusions blasted to smithereens. Legal entanglements.


Oct: The Hierophant R – I recall JMG saying this card R is often the card of tyranny – especially in spiritual matters. Vs Emperor R being tyranny of the material realm. So I’m thinking that whatever happens in Aug-Sep is going to let loose a whirlwind of civil unrest across the U.S. Quite possibly of BLM or Antifa-ish in nature. There is also the possibility of things like BLM vs Proud Boys clashes. Alt-Left duking it out with Alt-Right. Days and nights of angry mobs burning, looting and pillaging.

Unrest to the point many states will call out their national guard to put it down. MSM will be full of news about how domestic terrorism is at an all time high and Executive Orders (aka Royal Decrees) will be issued to declare a state of emergency. If you’re not for The Right Thing to Do that means you’re the domestic terrorist or terrorist-sympathizer that must be stopped. Congress will be such a basket case of gridlock they won’t be able to stop it. I think high-wealth elites might actually be scared for their own lives and begin flying out to a pre-prepared bunkers or some other hidey-hole. Hence another reason to emigrate and begin serious elite-wealth capital flight. Big Tech may get very bold in de-platforming, censoring and silencing anyone seen as a threat to the old-time status quo. Progress is dying so the response is…tyranny.

Nov: The Chariot R – Willpower defeated. The Chariot upright is the card of a premier, unstoppable, unified will. So it’s opposite is one of dispersion, dissipation, scattering and defeat. The people feeling numb, in shock, anxious and depressed. I’m guessing there will be a big spike in suicides and deaths from drug overdoses. Rampant upspiral in violent crime everywhere. I think it begins to hit that Progress *horror of horrors* might have just died. Even worse, they lived to see it and now what do they do? They didn’t die. The Apocalypse didn’t happen. The Rapture didn’t happen. What happens now that their “the shining beacon on a hill” is dead or dying? Every day in their mind stretches out into a grey, soulless, entropy they don’t understand and don’t know how to cope with. So they go numb, in a state of shock and can’t digest what has happened.

Right about here is where I think those IT Ukrainians will look around and think, “Good grief, this again? Shrug, then dust themselves off and have a leg up on native-born Americans on how to survive in uncertain times.

And here it is….the final card I turned over for America 2023

Dec: Death (A) – Yama – the card of death of all things that come to their natural end. Yama is an interesting death card. He’s more about death that is a natural end point. Not the kind of Kali-Shock-and-Awe death of Sri Arya’s other death arcana card. Kali as Death card is the shakti of Time’s Up! She gets transition done in a harsh way. Yama’s is more the shakti of closing a book on a story well-told.

This card turned over upright – which Sri Arya says is actually auspicious! When it turns over upright it is about

“karmic transformation, not personal extinction so do not fear. New beginnings after comprehensive endings…the clearing of karmic clutter so that life flows clearly and smoothly. This is usually painful but always necessary and ultimately you feel grateful. Shaw said, ‘Life levels all men. Death reveals the eminent.’ The disintegration you experience will be followed by renewals and infusions of energy. Outbursts of creativity and valuable work. Spiritually a time of immense possibility and progress, but the journey can be disconcerting and disorienting. Destabilising changes at work, surprising reorganizations. Unexpected rewards and promotions are possible.”

comments.title: Time Management
comments.content:
Hi John,

How do you manage your time? I am at mid life with few achievements and look at others who have accomplished so much. The number of books you have published plus what you have talked about doing in your past is one example to me. I originally looked at magic as a short cut, but don't see it that way anymore. Has magic helped you in being productive?

replies.title: Re: Time Management
replies.content: It's quite simple. I don't own a television, I don't play video games, and I figured out a quarter century ago how to support myself doing what I want to do, so I don't have to sacrifice lots of my time to an employer to keep the rent paid. I don't particularly manage my time -- I just avoid wasting it on things that don't matter. Each day, after my morning magical practices and breakfast, I look at the options and decide what I'm going to do that day, and then do it.

replies.title: Re: Time Management
replies.content: That's a nice schedule!

replies.title: Re: Time Management
replies.content:
FWIW I work on the basis that you always overestimate what you can do in a day and underestimate what you can do in a year. So those big achievements aren't all that big so long as you do a little a day and don't get hung up on how little you do each day. Eventually it all adds up (and often sooner than you'd think).

I'm working a full-time job and playing sport each week, but I've still managed to translate three books on the side this year and brewed about 8 all-grain batches of beer. Like JMG, I don't own a television and I don't play video games. Working from home has saved me about an hour a day that I can put towards my translation work. As the saying goes, there is no silver bullet. If there is a secret, it is not giving up.

comments.title: Respect for the Dead
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I just received word that one of my very elderly aunts died this evening. She is not someone I was close to; haven't seen her in nearly 30 years. My dealings with her immediate family, other than my mother who is estranged from her, is zilch.

Aunt was a totally committed Mormon. I'm a Heathen with an ancestor altar, at which I offer a cup of coffee and a general thanks and blessing to my ancestors each morning.

My instinct is to include my Mormon aunt as she is family, but in life I'm quite certain she would've been horrified at the prospect. Perhaps one's view of such things changes after death. Regardless, I bear her a general sort of good will, and want to do the right thing.

What might you advise?

Thank you!
OtterGirl

comments.title: Disconnection from a Magical Current
comments.content:
Good evening, JMG. I recently listened to a podcast where the guest, who stated he had been a member of a HOGD group in California (if that matters), butted heads with the the Order's "Ipsissimus", and was kicked out. Specifically, he was "reversed circumambulated" as a ritual to "excommunicate" and disconnect him from the Order's current and egregore so that he couldn't work "Golden Dawn" magic anymore.

I am curious if you are familiar with this, and if so, could say a thing or two about it in case someone who qualifies as a "putz" were to cause problems for an Order and could be removed that way.

As always, thank you for this forum and your sage counsel.

YITO,
/|\

replies.title: Re: Disconnection from a Magical Current
replies.content:
The only thing that would do is cut the member's connection to that particular branch of the HOGD. The initiatory effects of the work remain with the soul no matter what, and indeed are passed on to future incarnations; the guy could just have done a series of solitary workings to reaffirm his connection to the egregor of the tradition and he'd be fine.

It's quite easy to expel someone magically from an organization. It's effectively impossible to take away the effects of initiation -- though inevitably there are some people who insist they can do that. Magic unfortunately attracts such people now and then.
